skill.name: twitter-automation
skill.description: Automates Twitter/X account actions through the inference.sh belt CLI, including posts, media posts, likes, retweets, DMs, follows, profile lookup, and post lookup.
skill.summary: Run Twitter/X posting and engagement workflows through inference.sh belt apps.
skill.author: infsh-skills

security_audit.summary: Static analysis over-reported Ruby backtick execution and weak cryptography; those alerts are false positives from Markdown text and code formatting. Real risk remains because the skill authorizes belt CLI commands that call inference.sh and can perform authenticated Twitter/X actions such as posting, deleting, DMing, retweeting, and following.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated Twitter/X Actions Through External CLI
medium_findings.description: The skill allows Bash(belt *) and documents belt commands that can post, delete, like, retweet, send DMs, follow users, and query accounts. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but misuse can modify a connected Twitter/X account or create spam without careful user confirmation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration and concrete belt commands directly show authenticated account-changing actions. No evidence found of malicious intent, so the concern is misuse and side effects rather than malware.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party Network Service Dependency
medium_findings.description: The skill relies on inference.sh and belt app runs, which transmit prompts and action data to an external service. Users must trust that service and understand that connected Twitter/X integration data may be used to complete requested actions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation explicitly identifies inference.sh and includes belt app commands for remote apps. The exact transport and credential handling are not shown in this file, so the confidence is high but not absolute.

medium_findings.title: Social Platform Abuse and Compliance Risk
medium_findings.description: The documented apps include DMs, follows, likes, retweets, and posting workflows. These capabilities can violate platform rules or user expectations if run in bulk, automated without review, or used for unsolicited outreach.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The capability list and examples clearly include engagement and messaging actions. The file does not show bulk automation loops, so the risk depends on user workflow and operational safeguards.

low_findings.title: Static Backtick Execution Alerts Are Mostly Markdown Formatting
low_findings.description: The Ruby/shell backtick detections are not Ruby code execution. They occur in Markdown code fences and inline command examples, although the examples do include real belt shell commands that are covered separately as medium risk.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line-number review shows Markdown fences and inline code, not Ruby backtick execution syntax. The remaining shell command risk is real but is not the scanner-described Ruby execution pattern.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported weak-cryptography locations are the frontmatter description and a Markdown table header. No cryptographic algorithm or hashing operation appears at those lines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain descriptive text and table labels only. I did not find evidence of MD5, SHA-1, DES, or other weak cryptographic use in those locations.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Alerts Are Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to inference.sh documentation, installation instructions, an image, and a placeholder media URL. They are expected documentation references, not hidden exfiltration endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible Markdown links and a sample media URL. They do not include encoded secrets or evidence of covert data transfer.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash Tool Permission for belt Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill permits Bash(belt *), allowing an agent to run any belt subcommand. This broad permission is necessary for the documented workflows but expands the action surface beyond read-only guidance.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools line is explicit, and the file also suggests browsing all belt apps. The scope is limited to belt, which lowers severity compared with unrestricted shell access.

dangerous_patterns.title: Account-Mutating Social Media Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples include commands that create posts, delete posts, send DMs, follow users, like posts, and retweet. These are high-impact actions if run against the wrong account or content.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands and app IDs directly map to account-changing actions. The risk is contextual because the user must authenticate and choose inputs.

content.value_statement: Posting and engagement tasks on Twitter/X are repetitive and easy to mistime. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured prompts for running inference.sh belt apps.
actual_capabilities: Runs belt examples for posting text tweets through x/post-tweet.
Creates posts with media URLs through x/post-create.
Likes, retweets, deletes, and retrieves posts by tweet ID.
Sends direct messages and follows users with specific inputs.
Retrieves user profiles and links to inference.sh X documentation.
Combines image or video generation belt apps with X posting workflows.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh belt CLI and an authenticated account.
Does not provide its own Twitter/X API client or credential storage.
Cannot verify platform policy compliance before each action.
Media workflows require a reachable image or video URL.

use_cases.title: Publish Campaign Updates
use_cases.description: Draft and post concise Twitter/X updates with optional media URLs through approved belt commands.

use_cases.title: Manage Developer Announcements
use_cases.description: Post release notes, retrieve tweet details, and retweet project updates from a controlled workflow.

use_cases.title: Handle Account Engagement
use_cases.description: Like, retweet, follow, and send DMs after reviewing each target ID and message.

prompt_templates.title: Post a Simple Update
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a short Twitter/X post about this update, then show the belt command before running it: [update].

prompt_templates.title: Create a Media Post
prompt_templates.prompt: Prepare a Twitter/X post with this media URL. Keep the text under 240 characters and ask me to confirm before posting: [media URL].

prompt_templates.title: Review an Engagement Action
prompt_templates.prompt: Check this tweet ID and recommend whether to like, retweet, reply outside this skill, or take no action: [tweet ID].

prompt_templates.title: Build a Multi-Step Launch Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a launch sequence using approved X actions only. Include drafted post text, media inputs, review checkpoints, and the exact belt commands.

output_examples.input: Post a release announcement for a new analytics dashboard.
output_examples.output: The assistant drafts a concise announcement, asks for confirmation, and prepares a belt post command using the approved tweet text.

output_examples.input: Share this product image on X with a short caption.
output_examples.output: The assistant checks the media URL, writes a caption, and prepares a media post workflow for review before publishing.

output_examples.input: Send a thank-you DM to a specific user ID.
output_examples.output: The assistant writes a direct message, confirms the recipient ID, and prepares the DM action only after approval.

best_practices: Confirm every post, DM, delete, follow, like, and retweet before running belt.
Use test accounts or drafts when validating new social workflows.
Keep platform rules, consent, and account permissions visible during review.
anti_patterns: Do not run bulk engagement actions without human review.
Do not paste secrets, access tokens, or private customer data into post inputs.
Do not use the skill to send unsolicited DMs or manipulate engagement.

faq.question: Does this skill post directly to Twitter/X?
faq.answer: It guides and runs inference.sh belt apps that can post after the user authenticates and approves the action.

faq.question: Does it require the Twitter/X API?
faq.answer: Users configure the X integration through inference.sh. The skill itself does not implement a standalone API client.

faq.question: Can it schedule posts?
faq.answer: The description mentions scheduling, but the visible examples focus on immediate belt app actions.

faq.question: Can it upload media files?
faq.answer: The documented media workflow uses a media URL. It does not show direct local file upload.

faq.question: Is it safe for production accounts?
faq.answer: Use it only with explicit review steps because it can change public account state and send messages.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
